title: プロトコルの再現性、発見性を高め、よりユーザーフレンドリーにする方法
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"7e4k2","text":"protocols.ioでは、自由自在に詳細なメソッドを入力して表示できます。しかし、ここに書かれている簡単な提案は、あなたのプロトコルをあなたや他の人にとってより有用なものにするのに役立ちます。重要なのは、ここで紹介されている簡単なステップによって、あなたのプロトコルの可視性を大幅に向上させ、必要とする科学者がそれを見つけられるようになるということです。","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d7fiv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1k5kd","text":"ご不明な点がございましたら、お気軽にprotocols.ioチームinfo [at] protocols.ioまでご連絡ください。","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bk33g","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e3p4c","text":"このガイドは、以下の日本語訳です。","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5in14","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3o6oh","text":"Lenny Teytelman, protocols.io team, Anita Broellochs2020.How to make your protocol more reproducible, discoverable, and user-friendly.protocols.iohttps://dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bnknmcve","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":146,"length":49}],"data":[]},{"key":"6sjts","text":"Version created byLenny Teytelman","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":18,"length":15}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"78529EA896AA11EB8D0A0A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bnknmcve"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"78529F0196AA11EB8D0A0A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://www.protocols.io/researchers/lenny-teytelman8"}}}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"ak3b6","text":"The importance of detailed protocols for reproducibility.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":57}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cd01h","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d15om","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9jpv","text":"With the recent publication in eLife of the first studies from the Reproducibility Projects: Cancer Biology, there has been a lot of emphasis on the missing details in the Materials \u0026 Methods sections of published research papers. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":67,"length":40}],"data":[]},{"key":"9k24r","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bnte2","text":"Monya Baker \u0026 Elie Dolgin in Nature News:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":0,"length":40}],"data":[]},{"key":"7qq1e","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6giu6","text":"Perhaps the clearest finding from the project is that many papers include too few details about their methods, says Errington. Replication teams spent many hours working with the original authors to chase down protocols and reagents, in many cases because they had been developed by students and postdocs who were no longer with the lab. Even so, the final reports include long lists of reasons why the replication studies might have turned out differently — from laboratory temperatures to tiny variations in how a drug was delivered. If the project helps to bring such confusing details to the surface, it will have performed a great service, Errington says.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":660}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ei6p5","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"evtlv","text":"Ed Yong in The Atlantic:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":0,"length":24}],"data":[]},{"key":"2mimc","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"13r3p","text":"The hardest part, by far, was figuring out exactly what the original labs actually did. Scientific papers come with methods sections that theoretically ought to provide recipes for doing the same experiments. But often, those recipes are incomplete, missing out important steps, details, or ingredients. In some cases, the recipes aren’t described at all; researchers simply cite an earlier study that used a similar technique. “I’ve done it myself: you reference a previous paper and that one references a paper and that one references a paper, and now you’ve gone years and the methodology doesn’t exist,” admit Errington. “Most people looking at these papers wouldn’t even think of going through these steps. They’d just guess. If you asked 20 different labs to replicate a paper, you’d end up with 10 different methodologies that aren’t really comparable.”","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":860}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5ftsd","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"685db","text":"So, in every case, he had to ask the scientists behind the original experiments for the details of their work. Oftentimes, the person who actually did the experiments had left the lab, so an existing team member had to rummage through old notebooks or data files. The project ended up being hugely time-consuming for everyone concerned. “We spent a boatload of time trying to get back to ground zero,” says Errington.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":417}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4q9nc","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bgasq","text":"Julia Belluz, VOX: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":0,"length":17}],"data":[]},{"key":"fk2kj","text":"Scientists and journals need to get better at describing their methods and sharing data","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"45roh","text":"------------------------------","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7qroc","text":"It is not hard to make your protocol reproducible for the future you (when you try to repeat the experiment in 6 months) and for others (future members of your lab or company, other scientists after publication of your papers). Please see the simple suggestions in the 'Steps' section here and also read the excellent How to Write an Easily Reproducible Protocol from Arsalan Daudi.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":4,"offset":318,"length":44},{"key":5,"offset":368,"length":13}],"data":[]},{"key":"8gvut","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"416le","text":"(Arsalan's guide is cross-posted below with his permission.)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"btpt5","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8hanj","text":"How to Write an Easily Reproducible Protocol","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":44}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"edvml","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7g3l3","text":"SUMMARY","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cqjft","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5n779","text":"Research articles seldom describe experimental protocols in sufficient detail to reproduce them in another lab","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6comh","text":"Protocols should be thought of as stand-alone scientific articles that provide context and detail on the research methodology","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9mh9i","text":"There are a growing number of resources that provide the research community with access to high quality protocols","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"egng8","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8grns","text":"Have you ever looked at the Materials and Methods section of a research paper to try and find details of a protocol you want to implement in the lab? Have you come across short paragraphs that concisely summarize a method but leave out critical details? Have you found yourself asking questions like:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"c3e8n","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e908j","text":"How long did they spin those samples for?","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b3ge6","text":"Did they store the overnight suspension in a fridge or freezer?","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2m88e","text":"Exactly which ultra high metal affinity column did they purchase?","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"db6qc","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"62b33","text":"If you answered yes to any of these, don’t be alarmed – you are not alone.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"914hn","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9qqgf","text":"The art of writing a precise yet detailed and contextualized, relatable and reproducible protocol has far too often been confined to a lab notebook that will never again be seen by anybody other than the lab notebook owner and a handful of their colleagues.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"50lov","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5nid0","text":"This article will share some pointers that provide a framework for writing an easily reproducible protocol.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7p45q","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"es981","text":"Structure as a whole","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a52io","text":"It is important to think of a protocol (may it be wet or dry lab-based, biology or chemistry-focused, simple or advanced) as a brief, modular and self-contained scientific publication. To this end, several factors can be considered when preparing a draft.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9u7fi","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"82s7s","text":"Putting the methodology in context","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e08r9","text":"You never know who will refer to your protocol in the future. It may be a new graduate student joining the lab you’re preparing to leave or a new postdoc joining several years later when you’re a busy and established PI thousands of miles away possibly researching something entirely different. Alternatively, it may be a researcher you have never met who is working on a different species but looking for a protocol they can adapt for their needs. Whoever it may be, they will likely benefit from reading a three- to four-sentence abstract at the onset that briefly explains the system you worked on, describes any major obstacles or challenges you faced, and highlights the applicability of your workflow.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5nef5","text":" ","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"29s88","text":"Materials and tools that were used in the protocol","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"98dpl","text":"Lets face it: two different batches of agarose or of magnesium sulfate may not be the same. Commonly used biochemical reagents could vary in terms of purity, yield, pH, hydration state, grade, and possibly additional biochemical or biophysical features. For many researchers this may not matter; yet for others it may be a deal breaker in terms of implementing your protocol into their workflow.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9v9l6","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"27odm","text":"Similarly, when you mention “centrifuge,” this may not indicate how fast it can spin or how cold it can keep your samples. When a researcher finds out half way through an experiment that the process they set up based on your protocol cannot be completed because a simple detail was missing, the outcome is frustrating for everyone concerned.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7be7q","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5mc3o","text":"This can lead to a poor reflection of your protocol, wasted resources in the lab, and time and effort that could have been better utilized. Share as many details as you can, such as catalog numbers and manufacturer details.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"48p74","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7fv5s","text":"A chronology of steps","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3gkhs","text":"Bullet points are easy to follow and help others track a particular workflow. Always think about exactly what you did when you were using your protocol in the lab, and that the person using your protocol may be a novice experimenter.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8nrov","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"118q0","text":"Think about the quantities you used, sample replication, storage conditions, spinning and mixing, and details on how to operate various instruments and equipment. Listing all steps in a chronological and modular fashion can be a very effective interface for researchers that are trying to implement your protocol within their own laboratory workflows.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"298u2","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3v8kq","text":"Notes, recipes, tips, and tricks","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"c4e2l","text":"I would recommend wrapping up your protocol draft with a brief section that highlights details you might take for granted. Sometimes it is the little things that make an experiment work, flicking a tube gently versus vortexing it, spraying water on a leaf surface before infiltrating it with a syringe, or using PBS and tweezers to remove glue from a piece of sliced gel.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"20vrb","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"27e4t","text":"These are exactly the types of simple tricks or “secret sauce” that can save researchers weeks and potentially months of time and effort in the successful implementation of a protocol.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7tnbr","text":" ","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"89nir","text":"Conclusion","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"148mr","text":"A well-curated database of protocols can serve as the backbone of a high performing research laboratory. There are a growing number of resources such as Bio-protocol and protocols.io that allow you to share your protocols effectively with the research community and build your own portfolio of research methodologies.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":6,"offset":153,"length":12},{"key":7,"offset":170,"length":12}],"data":[]},{"key":"a6dq0","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"epphp","text":"Resources such as these as well as other established protocol journals and organizations such as JOVE, Science Exchange, COS, and GBSI are filling an important niche to ensure that the scientific community maintains its core mission of delivering reproducible results.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":8,"offset":97,"length":4},{"key":9,"offset":103,"length":16},{"key":10,"offset":121,"length":3},{"key":11,"offset":130,"length":4}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"69509D74F8174EDA8539E8DA621D44E1","url":"https://elifesciences.org/collections/reproducibility-project-cancer-biology"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"9C591E27C8764F588F8264981174FE2A","url":"http://www.nature.com/news/cancer-reproducibility-project-releases-first-results-1.21304?WT.mc_id=TWT_NatureNews"}},"10":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"8F4641F51AAA4F6DB338206FB7BE52E5","url":"https://cos.io/"}},"11":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"A8C553977F604BB3A5806B9CAE4CE5B5","url":"http://www.gbsi.org/"}},"2":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"FDA2602556614F4193AD8FB9B0D81B2B","url":"https://www.theatlantic.com/science/archive/2017/01/what-proportion-of-cancer-studies-are-reliable/513485/"}},"3":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"10332E6BFA2E4F0CA9816F0668D39FB9","url":"http://www.vox.com/science-and-health/2017/1/23/14324326/replication-science-is-hard"}},"4":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"7B13114A8F8A49919844A3CDE68AAA70","url":"http://www.aje.com/en/arc/how-to-write-an-easily-reproducible-protocol/"}},"5":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"CA673CAD3DCA4936A5D3D0B3612F0C3B","url":"http://www.aje.com/en/arc/authors/arsalan-daudi/"}},"6":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"AAA3AF58C94A452ABE72B7F1FA374631","url":"http://www.bio-protocol.org/Default.aspx"}},"7":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"58C966C8EFB14A9495DAD73A9744E6B4","url":"https://www.protocols.io/"}},"8":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"78E34900736E43AEA393EC3F76398874","url":"http://www.jove.com/"}},"9":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"16F494A0137E41DFA2EA9C98527F5D93","url":"https://www.scienceexchange.com/"}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"fp2go","text":"もし始めたばかりであれば、まずは How to create a new protocol ガイドをご覧になると良いかもしれません。","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":17,"length":28}],"data":[]},{"key":"60hjv","text":" ","type":"header-two","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"004202280EE84542BEF20DD9CCE33C0D","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/how-to-create-a-new-protocol-fngbmbw?step=1"}}}}
steps: タイトル: 原稿を書くときのように、タイトルは説明的にしましょう。これは、Googleやprotocols.ioでの検索に役立ちます。
サムネイル画像: プロトコルを識別するための画像があれば、自分や他のユーザーが使用するプロトコルのリストからそのプロトコルを見つけやすくなります。
グループ: あなたが参加した関連グループにあなたのプロトコルを追加すると、そのグループのメンバーに通知が届き、あなたのプロトコルを見ることができるようになります。これにより、あなたのプロトコルを適切な人に見せることができるようになります。
キーワード: プロトコルを入力する際、「description」タブにキーワードを追加することができます。このキーワードは、プロトコルの「metadata」タブに表示され、そのキーワードを含むクエリの検索結果にプロトコルが表示されます。
コンポーネント: 各ステップでは、右側のボックスをクリックして、これらのコンポーネントを追加し、重要な詳細情報を提供することができます。
ウェットラボのコンポーネント : ベンダー名、カタログ番号、リンク、RRIDなどで、試薬や装置を特定することは非常に重要です。使用した抗体の名前だけでは不十分です。なぜなら、ベンダー間、さらにはロット間のばらつきが実験の成否を左右するからです。ステップの中や「materials」セクションにある「regent」コンポーネントを使用してください。
バイオインフォマティクスのコンポーネント: 「command」、 「software package」、「dataset」、「external link」を使ってください。 コンポーネントを使用して、コード、ソフトウェアのバージョン、分析に使用するデータセットの場所とバージョンを指定します。
画像/ビデオ : プロトコルップのステップ（またはガイドライン）にも、写真やビデオを追加することができます。これにより、誰かがあなたのメソッドを初めて試すときに、大きな違いをもたらすことができます。
実験を行う際には、このプラットフォームのプロトコルに沿って、「run」モードで段階的に行うことができます。
シングル・ステップの説明: メソッドの詳細をレシピのようなチェックリスト形式に分割します。「4分間インキュベートした後、RTで5分間遠心分離する」ではなく、以下のように分けます。

繰り返すステップ: 「3回洗う」や「8～13の手順を繰り返す」ではなく、それぞれの手順を明記します。 これは、プロトコルを「実行」（running）するときに役立ちます。同じことを繰り返していると、何回やったかわからなくなることがよくありました。今、私は洗浄のステップの2/4をやっているのか、3/4をやっているのか。 洗浄が多すぎるとサンプルを失うことになり、洗浄が少なすぎるとコンタミネーションが発生します…
簡潔に: インストラクションステップの文字数が多すぎると、理解し辛くなります。コメントやヒントは、「annotation」コンポーネントに移動します。
時間を計るステップ: 必要に応じて、個々のステップで「Duration/Timer」コンポーネントを使用します。これにより、プロトコルを 「実行 」する際にタイマーを使用することが可能になります。 プロトコルの実行：
セクション: 連続するステップに同じ「セクションタイトル」をつけることで、関連するステップをセクションにまとめます。

ガイドライン、注意事項、始める前に: 「Guidelines & Warnings」タブを使用して、「始める前」の指示や警告を追加します。また、「Guidelines & Warnings」タブには、メソッド、参考文献、コツなどの情報を入力するための「Guideline」フィールドがあります。プロトコルを「機密」、または「取り扱いに注意が必要な内容を含む」としてラベルを付けるオプションも用意されています。

安全性: 「Guidelines & Warnings」タブに 「Safety Warings」フィールドがあります。また、「Safety information」コンポーネントを使用して、特定の試薬や機器に関するステップレベルの安全性情報を追加することもできます。これらの警告は冗長にするのが良いので、関連性のあるステップ、「warnings」、「Guideline」に入れることを検討してください。

あなたのプロトコルの再現性を高めるために、 ガイドライン をご覧ください。 また、上記の例で使われている、Protocols.ioの利用者による詳細なプロトコルもご覧ください: Staining protocol for unicellular protists: Mitotracker and Dapi , by Maria Rubio Iron Chloride Precipitation of Viruses from Seawater , Matthew Sullivan lab QIIME:Moving Pictures of the human microbiome , Bonnie Hurwitz High quality DNA from Fungi for long read sequencing e.g. PacBio , Benjamin Schwessinger Near-Infrared (NIR) Western Blot Detection (PVDF membrane) , LI-COR Biosciences Functional calibration for trunk and lower limb fixed inertial sensors , Benedikt Fasel
creator: Ayu Nagane Uchikawa
